IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—measuring and characterizing reading-comprehension dataset difficulty—by grounding two metric classes in psycholinguistic theory and refining a 13-skill prerequisite taxonomy (Section 3.1) and readable linguistic features (Vajjala & Meurers). Empirical annotation of six major RC datasets (Section 4) and analyses (e.g., sentence-distance statistics in Table 6 and the finding that readability does not directly predict question difficulty) provide actionable insights for dataset construction, curriculum learning, and evaluation. These contributions are likely to influence how researchers design and analyze RC benchmarks and open avenues for targeted dataset creation and diagnostic evaluation, with potential applications beyond RC (e.g., text simplification and educational assessment).

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well organized (Related Work → Metrics → Annotation → Discussion) and clearly states its main contribution early (adoption of two classes of metrics: prerequisite skills and readability), and the annotation procedure is described concretely (Section 4.1). However, the provided text contains frequent repetition and formatting artifacts (many paragraphs such as “Our two classes of metrics…” and the prerequisite-skills list appear duplicated), stray LaTeX/footnote markers, and some awkward sentence breaks that hinder readability (e.g., repeated lines around Table 6 and Figure 1). Figures/tables and examples are informative but their presentation is rough/fragmented in this extract, and occasional grammatical/formatting issues make parts hard to follow without reformatting.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states claims (two metric classes, a 13-skill taxonomy, and dataset annotations) and reports dataset analyses (e.g., Table 6), but the provided text lacks key experimental details and statistical support: sample sizes per dataset, inter-annotator agreement, and significance testing are not reported. The sentence-distance calculation is ambiguously defined (“sum of distances of any two sentences” for >2 sentences) and may be flawed. The methodology admits limitations (cannot evaluate sentence-selection competence) and several assumptions (e.g., number of required skills maps to question difficulty) are asserted without corroborating analysis in the excerpt, so conclusions are only partially justified.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — this paper is squarely within the scope of top NLP venues: it proposes evaluation metrics for reading-comprehension datasets, refines prerequisite skill categories, and applies them to six standard RC datasets (e.g. SQuAD, MCTest) via a documented annotation procedure (Section 4.1). The work uses established linguistic/readability features (Vajjala & Meurers 2012) and psycholinguistic theory (Kintsch; McNamara & Magliano) to produce dataset-analysis metrics and discusses implications for dataset construction and system development, which matches the audience and evaluation/resource standards expected at ACL/EMNLP. Positioning and citations to relevant prior work (SQuAD, QA4MRE, Chen et al. 2016, Sugawara et al.) show it is correctly situated in the RC/dataset analysis literature.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a coherent, useful synthesis rather than a radical new method: it combines established cognitive theory (McNamara & Magliano; Kintsch) with readability features (Vajjala & Meurers) and refines Sugawara et al.’s prerequisite-skill taxonomy from 10 to 13 (e.g., adding ellipsis, bridging, elaboration, meta-knowledge) and applies this annotation across six RC datasets. The contribution is clearly differentiated from prior work ( Sections 3.1 and 4 describe the refinements and dataset annotation) and involves non-trivial engineering/analysis, but it mainly extends and integrates existing ideas and metrics rather than introducing a fundamentally new problem formulation or technique—hence moderate novelty.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper applies its proposed metrics to six RC datasets and provides dataset analyses (annotation procedure with three annotators; Table 6 reporting average number and distance of required sentences; Section 6 discussion of corpus genre and question type), supporting some claims. It explicitly discusses limitations and annotation issues (disagreements, unsolvable questions, inability to evaluate sentence-selection competence). However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, inter-annotator agreement and exact sampling sizes/guidelines are not provided, and reproducibility details are limited, so the experimental validation is adequate but misses important analyses and replicability information.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper systematically compares its proposed metrics to prior work and datasets: it builds on Sugawara et al. (2017) for prerequisite skills and Vajjala & Meurers (2012) for readability (Section 3 and Section 4.1), and applies the same annotation procedure across six RC datasets (Table 6) for direct comparison. It also cites and discusses relevant competing resources and limitations (e.g., Pitler & Nenkova not usable due to missing discourse annotations; Chen et al. (2016) on unsolvable CNN/DailyMail questions), and explains differences from prior classifications (Kintsch, McNamara & Magliano). While comparisons are fair and thoughtful, the paper mainly analyzes datasets/metrics rather than benchmarking against recent algorithmic baselines or reporting side-by-side performance where applicable, hence one minor omission.
